coredata.dc:title: WO3 nanosheets with peroxidase-like activity and carbon dots based ratiometric fluorescent strategy for xanthine oxidase activity sensing and inhibitor screening
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Xinxin
Liu, Weiping
Li, Yue
Zhu, Wanglisha
Liu, Hongmei
Wen, Yulu
Bai, Ruyu
Luo, Xiaojun
Zhang, Guoqi
Zhao, Yan
coredata.dc:description: The abnormal level of xanthine oxidase (XOD) often causes pathological changes, which are related to a series of diseases. Herein, a novel and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform based on WO3 nanosheets and carbon dots (CDs) was constructed to detect XOD activity for the first time. Under the catalytic oxidation of xanthine by XOD, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was generated. In the presence of H2O2, WO3 nanosheets were able to catalyze the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine to generate 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with a yellow fluorescence signal at 570 nm due to its great peroxidase-like activity. The oxidation product DAP was capable of quenching the fluorescence of CDs at 430 nm through the inner filter effect. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity ratio F570/F430 can be used for quantitative analysis of XOD activity. This assay displayed good linear relationships in the range of 0.005–0.05 U/L and 0.5–40 U/L with a detection limit of 0.002 U/L. In addition, this ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform was successfully applied to the determination of XOD in human serum samples and XOD inhibitor screening, demonstrating significant potential in disease diagnosis and drug-screening applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: WO3 nanosheets
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Xanthine oxidase
Inhibitor screening

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and properties of the CDs
Characterization and peroxidase-like activity of WO3 nanosheets
Kinetic assay and catalytic mechanism of WO3 nanosheets
Construction of CDs and WO3 nanosheets based ratiometric system

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by west light foundation of the Chinese academy of sciences (to YZ), and the Science and Technology Programs of Sichuan Province of China (No. 2021YFS0060 and No. 2023NSFSC0637 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) the size distribution histogram, (C) XRD spectrum, and (D) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (E) UV–Vis absorption spectrum, excitation spectrum at λex = 350 nm, and emission spectrum at λem = 430 nm of CDs. Insert: photograph of CDs under the excitation of white light (Left) and UV lamp with 365 nm (Right). (F) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with the excitation wavelength increasing from 300 to 380 nm.
(A) TEM image, (B) the size distribution histogram, (C) AFM image, and (D) the height profile of WO3 nanosheets.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of OPD, OPD/H2O2, OPD/WO3, OPD/H2O2/WO3, and OPD/H2O2/UA/WO3 system, respectively. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of WO3/OPD, WO3/OPD/H2O2, WO3/TMB, WO3/TMB/H2O2, respectively. Inset were the corresponding photos under visible light. Concentration: 0.1 M OPD, 2 μM H2O2, 0.636 mg/mL WO3, 10 mM UA, and 0.1 mM TMB.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, DAP and CDs/DAP, respectively. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of OPD and DAP, and excitation-emission spectra of CDs. (C) The fluorescence decay curve of CDs in the absence and presence of DAP. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/XAN/OPD, CDs/XOD/OPD, CDs/XOD/XAN/OPD and CDs/XOD/XAN/WO3/OPD system, respectively. Concentration: 1 mg/mL CDs, 20 mM DAP, 0.1 M OPD, 10 mM XAN, 60 U/L XOD, and 0.636 mg/mL WO3.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of XOD and XAN. (B) The plots of F570/F430 versus the concentration of XOD. Inset: the corresponding linear relationship of F570/F430 versus XOD concentration in the range of 0.005–0.05 U/L and 0.5–40 U/L, respectively. (C)

Anti-interference of CDs/OPD/WO3/XAN/XOD system and selectivity of CDs/OPD/WO3/XAN system for XOD activity analysis, respectively. Concentration: 1 mg/mL CDs, 10 mM XAN, 0.1 M OPD, 0.636 mg/mL WO3, 0.01 M metal ions, H2O2 and UA, 10 mg/mL amino acids, 10 mg/mL AA, Ade, Gua, GLU and HAS, and 10 U/mL other biomolecules.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of allopurinol, XAN (10 mM), and XOD (20 U/L). (B) The linear relationship of inhibition ratio versus the logarithm of allopurinol concentration (0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, and 150 μM). Concentration: 1 mg/mL CDs, 0.1 M OPD, and 0.636 mg/mL WO3.
Schematic illustration of the ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategy for XOD activity sensing.

List of XOD levels in serum samples of patients with hyperuricemia and normal people for contrast (n = 3)
a
.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data analysis was performed on independent samples with equal variances (***P < 0.001).

head.ce:title: WO3 nanosheets with peroxidase-like activity and carbon dots based ratiometric fluorescent strategy for xanthine oxidase activity sensing and inhibitor screening

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Zigong First People's Hospital, Zigong, 643000, Sichuan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Zigong First People's Hospital, Zigong 643000, Sichuan, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xinxin Jiang and Weiping Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The abnormal level of xanthine oxidase (XOD) often causes pathological changes, which are related to a series of diseases. Herein, a novel and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform based on WO3 nanosheets and carbon dots (CDs) was constructed to detect XOD activity for the first time. Under the catalytic oxidation of xanthine by XOD, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was generated. In the presence of H2O2, WO3 nanosheets were able to catalyze the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine to generate 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with a yellow fluorescence signal at 570 nm due to its great peroxidase-like activity. The oxidation product DAP was capable of quenching the fluorescence of CDs at 430 nm through the inner filter effect. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity ratio F570/F430 can be used for quantitative analysis of XOD activity. This assay displayed good linear relationships in the range of 0.005–0.05 U/L and 0.5–40 U/L with a detection limit of 0.002 U/L. In addition, this ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform was successfully applied to the determination of XOD in human serum samples and XOD inhibitor screening, demonstrating significant potential in disease diagnosis and drug-screening applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Xanthine oxidase (XOD), an important metalloflavoprotein, catalyzes the oxidation of xanthine (XAN) to uric acid (UA) in the presence of oxygen while simultaneously producing hydrogen peroxide [1]. Disorder of UA excretion may cause gout and hyperuricemia [2]. Inhibition of XOD activity is useful for treating hyperuricemia and related diseases [3,4]. However, synthetic XOD inhibitors such as febuxostat and allopurinol, which are widely applied in the treatment of gout, may cause serious side effects, including impairment of liver and kidney function and hypersensitivity syndrome [5–7]. For this reason, exploring safe and high-efficiency XOD inhibitors from medicinal plants is necessary for treating gout or other XOD-induced diseases [8]. Thus, the development of sensitive and facile approaches for XOD analysis and inhibitor screening has attracted great attention in clinical diagnosis and drug research. So far, various analytical approaches have been proposed for the detection of XOD, including liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [9], colorimetry [10], phosphorescence [11], and fluorimetry [12,13]. Among them, the fluorescence method stands out due to its simplicity, specificity, high sensitivity, and quick response [14]. For instance, Chang et al. prepared a Zr-based metal-organic framework (BTB-MOF) as a sensor and realized XOD activity analysis and XOD inhibitor screening by utilizing the influence of the enzymatic oxidation system on the fluorescence intensity of BTB-MOF [12]. Sidhu et al. successfully detected XOD concentration in an aqueous system using naphthalimide-based Turn-On fluorescence probe, enabling a low detection limit of 0.7 U/mL [13]. However, most of these fluorescence methods are single response signals, which are readily perturbed by external conditions, including instrument parameters, operation, measurement, etc. [15]. Compared with single fluorescence signal output, the ratiometric fluorescent method can greatly reduce external interference and effectively normalize the variation by detecting the analyte through the ratio of fluorescence intensities of two different emission wavelengths, which can greatly improve the accuracy and precision of detection. It is therefore of great significance to establish a facile, reliable, and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent strategy for the detection of XOD.

As a promising carbon-based nanomaterial, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have drawn great attention owing to their superiority over noble metal nanoclusters, traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots including low toxicity, good photostability, good biocompatibility, and excellent water dispersibility [16,17]. In recent years, CDs have been preferentially chosen as luminescent fluorophores to develop novel fluorescent sensors for enzymes such as acetylcholinesterase [18], tyrosinase [19], α-glucosidase [20], and thrombin [21]. However, the novel CDs-based assay for XOD has rarely been reported up to date.

Nanozymes, a series of nanomaterials with enzyme-like properties, have been regarded as favorable substitutes for traditional natural enzymes [22]. Compared with natural enzymes, nanozymes possess the advantages such as low cost, good catalytic activity, easy preparation, and high stability and durability [23–25]. Since the discovery of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with inherent peroxidase-like activity in 2007 [26], a variety of nanozymes, such as carbon structures-based nanozyme [27–29], metal-based nanozyme [30], metal oxides-based nanozyme [31,32], metal-organic frameworks (MOF) [33–35] have been developed in a wide range of fields including clinical diagnosis and treatment, optical sensors, as well as environmental engineering [36–39]. As a typical transition metal oxide material, tungsten oxide (WOx) has recently been applied in electrochromic devices, photoelectrochemistry, pH sensors, and so on due to its intricate structural properties [40]. However, poor solubility of WOx in aqueous solutions limits its applications in biological systems. It has been reported that hydrated tungsten oxides with layered structures can be easily exfoliated into two-dimensional nanosheets in water, which could significantly increase the solubility of WOx in aqueous solutions [41]. Thus, we expect that this type of two-dimensional nanosheets can exhibit excellent peroxidase-like activity for constructing sensing platforms for XOD determination in complex biological systems.

Herein, we developed a sensitive assay for XOD activity based on fluorescent CDs and WO3 nanosheets with peroxidase-like activity by applying ratiometric fluorometry for the first time (Scheme 1 ). During the determination, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was produced via the oxidation of XAN catalyzed by XOD. In the presence of H2O2, WO3 nanosheets catalyzed the oxidation of non-fluorescent o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to produce 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with a yellow fluorescence signal at 570 nm. At the same time, DAP was capable of quenching the fluorescence of CDs at 430 nm through the inner filter effect (IFE). Consequently, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for the determination of XOD activity was constructed. This method was successfully applied to the detection of XOD activity in human serum samples, showing good accuracy. Moreover, XOD inhibitor screening was further assessed using allopurinol as an example. Therefore, this work provides a novel, sensitive, and reliable platform for XOD activity assay and inhibitor screening based on CDs and WO3 nanosheets.

Tripterygium wilfordii Hook. f., Folium Nelumbinis, Terminalia chebula Retz., Dendranthema morifolium, Pueraria montana, Crataegus pinnatifida, and Curcuma phaeocaulis Valeton were supplied by Chengdu branch of Beijing Tong Ren Tang (Chengdu, China). Levodopa, sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminoethane (Tris), 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), OPD, DAP, H2O2, superoxide dismutase (SOD), histidine (His), catalase (CAT), isopropanol (IPA), L-terephthalic acid (TA), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrolin-1-oxide (DMPO), UA, and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). XOD, XAN, allopurinol, human serum albumin (HSA), L-cysteine (L-Cys), glycine (Gly), tyrosine (Tyr), lysine (Lys), adenine (Ade), guanine (Gua), glutathione (GSH), glucose, α-glucosidase (α-Glu), and polyphenol oxidase (PPO) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Normal human serum samples were purchased from Beijing Solar Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Serum samples of patients with hyperuricemia and the normal control group were provided by Zigong First People's Hospital.

Fluorescence spectra were measured through a HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Japan), and lifetimes were determined using an Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK). UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded by an FEI Talos F200X instrument (FEI, USA). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed on a Dimension FastScan atomic force microscope (Bruker Corporation, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were acquired on a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were acquired on an ESCALAB 250Xi instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). All Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements were acquired on a MS-5000X spectrometer (Magnettech, Germany).

CDs were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Firstly, 0.18 g of levodopa and 0.05 g of Tris were added into ultrapure water (10.0 mL) to obtain a clear solution under vigorous stirring. Thereafter, the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon stainless steel autoclave and kept heated at 180 °C for 8 h (Fig. S1). Subsequently, the product was collected and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to separate any insoluble solids. After that, the final solution was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with 1000 MWCO for 48 h to remove the precursors and resulting small molecules. At last, the obtained CDs solution was dried by a freezing dryer for further research.

The WO3 nanosheets were synthesized according to the previous study with some modification [41]. In brief, 400 mg of Na2WO4·2H2O was added to 300 mL nitric acid solution (4.8 M). After stirring for 72 h, the yellow solid was collected, rinsed with ultrapure water for three times, and then freeze-dried in a lyophilizer. The obtained WO3·2H2O (50 mg) was dispersed in 50 mL ultrapure water, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 24 h. The supernatant containing WO3 nanosheets was collected after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The as-prepared WO3 nanosheets with a concentration of 0.636 mg/mL were finally stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

For the detection of XOD, 100 μL of XAN (10 mM) solution and 200 μL of XOD solution at different concentrations were mixed and incubated at 35 °C for 30 min. Then, 50 μL of OPD (0.1 M) and 200 μL of WO3 nanosheets (0.636 mg/mL) were successively added to the above solution and incubated at 35 °C in the dark for 30 min. Thereafter, 1 mL of CDs (1 mg/mL) solution was added and incubated at room temperature for 2 min. The fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture solution were recorded at 350 nm excitation, and the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 430 nm and 570 nm (F570/F430) was used for quantitative analysis.

Allopurinol, an efficient XOD inhibitor and a commonly used drug for hyperuricemia and gout treatment, was selected as an example to verify the feasibility of this screening strategy. The procedure was carried out as follows. A 100 μL of allopurinol aqueous solution at different concentrations (from 0.1 μM to 150.0 μM) was mixed with 200 μL of XOD (20 U/L), and incubated at room temperature for 10 min. After that, 100 μL of XAN solution (10 mM) was added and incubated at 35 °C for 30 min. The resultant solutions containing allopurinol, XOD, and XAN were separately added into the analytical solution containing WO3 nanosheets (0.636 mg/mL), OPD (0.1 M), and CDs (1 mg/mL). The fluorescence emission spectra of the resulting mixtures were measured at 350 nm excitation. The half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) was defined as the inhibitor concentration with an inhibition ratio equal to 50. The IC50 value of a drug can be obtained from a dose-response regression plot with GraphPad Prism version 8 software and examining the influence of different concentrations of drug on XOD activity.

Moreover, the inhibitory abilities of Tripterygium wilfordii Hook. f., Folium Nelumbinis, Terminalia chebula Retz., Dendranthema morifolium, Pueraria montana, Crataegus pinnatifida, and Curcuma phaeocaulis Valeton were further investigated by the screening strategy. The extraction procedure of medicinal plants was carried out according to the following steps. Firstly, 1 g dried powder of medicinal plants were sonicated in 80% ethanol (40 mL) for 60 min, respectively. After that, the mixture was filtered and evaporated to obtain a dry extract, and finally diluted with PBS (50 mM, pH 6.5) for the following evaluation of inhibitory abilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and properties of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 A, TEM image revealed that the as-prepared CDs exhibited a spherical morphology and excellent mono-dispersity. Fig. 1B showed that the CDs displayed a relatively uniform size distribution of about 0.4–3.9 nm with an average diameter of about 2.37 nm. XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 1C) exhibited a broad diffraction peak (2θ) centered at about 22.12° which was attributed to disordered carbon atoms with the graphite lattice spacing (002 planes).

The surface groups of the CDs were displayed in FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1D). The wide peak at 3426 cm−1 corresponded to the O–H/N–H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 2926, 1620, and 1548 cm−1 were classified as the stretching vibrations of C–H, C=O, and C=C, respectively. Bending vibrations of C–N and C–H localized at about 1475 and 1396 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 1291 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of C–O. The FT-IR data indicated that the as-synthesized CDs were endowed with excellent water solubility due to the surface hydrophilic groups including amino, hydroxy, and carboxyl.

The structure information of CDs was further carried out by XPS (Fig. S2). The full range XPS analysis (Fig. S2A) displayed three dominant peaks at 284.80, 400.38, and 531.64 eV, which were ascribed to the binding energies of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum exhibited three major peaks that corresponded to C–C/C=C (284.8 eV), C–O (285.9 eV), and C=N (286.5 eV), respectively (Fig. S2B). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum also presented three binding energies peaks that were assigned to C–N–C (399.8 eV), N–H (400.3 eV), and N-(C)3 (401.5 eV), respectively (Fig. S2C). Additionally, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum showed three peaks at 531.8, 532.4, and 533.2 eV, which were attributed to C=O, C–OH/C–O–C, and –COOH, respectively (Fig. S2D). All the results of XPS were in agreement with that of FT-IR.

The optical properties of the as-synthesized CDs were verified by UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra. The CDs exhibited two characteristic UV–Vis absorption bands at 280 nm and 350 nm, which were ascribed to the π→π* electronic transition of aromatic C=C bond and n→π* transition of C=O and C=N bonds in the CDs structure, respectively (Fig. 1E). The optimal excitation and emission of CDs were located at 350 and 430 nm, respectively (Fig. 1E). In addition, the light-yellow aqueous solution of CDs exhibited blue fluorescence with UV light of 365 nm (insert in Fig. 1E). Moreover, the CDs displayed excitation-independent emission with a wide excitation band in the range of 300–380 nm (Fig. 1F), suggesting the CDs with a highly homogeneous surface structure and a relatively uniform particle size distribution [42].

The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured as 16.8% by using quinine sulfate as the reference (QY 54%) (Fig. S3A), which was comparable to previous reports [43]. Meanwhile, the mean fluorescence lifetime of CDs was calculated as 4.50 ns under excitation of 350 nm (Fig. S3B). The stability test of the CDs in Fig. S4 showed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs had no obvious change even under 365 nm UV light exposure for 180 min (Fig. S4A), wide pH range of 3–11 (Fig. S4B), and high ionic strength (Fig. S4C). All the results confirmed that the as-synthesized CDs possessed favorable stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and peroxidase-like activity of WO3 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Fig. 2 A, the WO3 nanosheets showed sheet-like morphology. Meanwhile, the sizes of WO3 nanosheets ranged from 12 nm to 32 nm with a mean particle size of 19.9 nm (Fig. 2B). The AFM image and the corresponding height profile revealed that the thickness of WO3 nanosheets was about 14–19 nm (Fig. 2C and D).

The full-range XPS spectrum of WO3 nanosheets was displayed in Fig. S5A. Two major peaks were assigned to tungsten (W) and oxygen (O) elements. Two additional peaks of carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) were due to the absorption of CO2 and the small amount of residual NO3 −, respectively. The high-resolution W 4f spectrum exhibited two major peaks at 35.70 and 37.95 eV, which were attributed to W 4f 7/2, and W 4f 5/2, respectively (Fig. S5B) [44]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum displayed four major peaks at 530.8 eV, 531.2 eV, 532.1 eV, and 533.1 eV, corresponding to the lattice oxygen (W–O), the oxygen species adsorbed in the form of O–C on the surface of WO3, hydroxyl oxygen, and the surface-adsorbed H2O, respectively (Fig. S5C) [45]. The XPS patterns of bulk WO3 and WO3 nanosheets were compared in Fig. S5D. The peak of (020) plane of WO3 nanosheets disappeared, indicating that the layer-by-layer structure was disrupted after ultrasonic treatment [41].

The peroxidase-like activity of WO3 nanosheets was studied completely by selecting the OPD as the substrate of peroxidase. As presented in Fig. 3 A, WO3 nanosheets can catalyze the oxidation of non-fluorescent OPD to generate fluorescent DAP with a yellow fluorescence signal at 570 nm in the presence of H2O2. On the contrary, the control group in the absence of H2O2 showed a weak fluorescence emission peak, and the control group in the absence of WO3 nanosheets exhibited almost no fluorescence signal. In addition, XOD can catalyze the oxidation of XAN to generate UA and H2O2. Considering the reducing ability of UA, the influence of UA on the peroxidase-like activity of WO3 nanosheet was studied. The fluorescence intensity of OPD/H2O2/WO3 nanosheets system was almost unchanged after adding UA, indicating that the influence of UA on the peroxidase-like activity of WO3 nanosheets can be ignored. Moreover, as displayed in Fig. 3B, in the presence of H2O2, WO3 nanosheets can catalyze the oxidation of OPD to form a yellow solution with a strong absorption peak at 430 nm (line b in Fig. 3B). In contrast, the color of the reaction system was almost colorless without H2O2. Additionally, another peroxidase substrate, such as TMB, was used to further verify the peroxidase-like activity of WO3 nanosheets. After adding H2O2, the color of the TMB/WO3 nanosheets system changed from colorless to green with characteristic absorption at 650 nm (line d in Fig. 3B). All these results confirmed the inherent peroxidase-like activity of WO3 nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic assay and catalytic mechanism of WO3 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Steady-state kinetic analysis was carried out to study the characteristics of the peroxidase-like activity of WO3 nanosheets with TMB and H2O2 as substrates (Fig. S6). For kinetic assays, only the concentration of one substrate was changed while the concentration of the other substrate was fixed. The kinetic data, maximum velocity (V max) and Michaelis−Menten constant (K m) can be determined by the Lineweaver-Burk equation, 1/v = (K m/V max) × (1/[S]) + 1/V max, where K m represents the Michaelis constant, V max and v are the maximal and the initial reaction velocity, respectively, and [S] represents the concentration of the substrate (H2O2 or TMB) concentration. Furthermore, the double-reciprocal curves of initial reaction velocity versus substrate concentration displayed parallel lines (Figs. S6C and D), suggesting a ping-pong mechanism [46]. As displayed in Table S1, the K m values of WO3 nanosheets to the substrates H2O2 and TMB (0.0037 and 0.0097 M, respectively) were lower than those of horse radish peroxidase (HRP), demonstrating that the stronger affinity of WO3 nanosheets towards H2O2 and TMB than those of HRP.

Subsequently, to estimate the catalytic mechanism of WO3 nanosheets, the catalytic reaction was studied in the presence of different scavengers of reactive oxygen species. Different concentrations of SOD (10, 50, 100 U/mL), His (0.05, 0.1, 1 mM), CAT (10, 50, 100 U/mL), and IPA (0.05, 0.1, 1 mM) were applied to capture super oxygen free radical (·O2 −), singlet oxygen (1O2), H2O2, and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), respectively [47]. As displayed in Fig. S7A, SOD, His and CAT had no influence on the absorbance of the system, while the absorbance (at 650 nm) constantly decreased with the increase of IPA concentration. These results suggested the catalytic mechanism of WO3 nanosheets involved the production of ·OH. In addition, TA was employed as a fluorescence probe to further verify the formation of ·OH. As displayed in Fig. S7B, the nonfluorescent TA was able to be oxidized by ·OH to produce fluorescent 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid in the presence of H2O2, and the fluorescent intensity constantly increased with the increase of WO3 nanosheets in amount, indicating that WO3 nanosheets can greatly accelerate the breakdown of H2O2 to ·OH. The generation of ·OH was also directly validated by EPR data with DMPO as a spin-trapping reagent. As displayed in Fig. S7C, no obvious EPR signal was detected in the mixed solution of DMPO with H2O2 or WO3 nanosheets. In contrast, four equidistant signals with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 were observed in the DMPO–H2O2–WO3 system. These results suggested that the catalytic performance of WO3 nanosheets indeed originated from the formation of ·OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of CDs and WO3 nanosheets based ratiometric system
ce:section.ce:para: The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs by DAP was studied. As displayed in Fig. 4 A, CDs and DAP exhibited fluorescence emission peaks at 430 and 570 nm, respectively. Nevertheless, the fluorescence of CDs was significantly quenched by DAP, and the strong fluorescence emission peak of DAP emerged at 570 nm.

The quenching mechanism of DAP on the fluorescence of CDs was further studied by carrying out absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetime measurements. As displayed in Fig. 4B, the absorption spectrum of DAP exhibited an obvious overlap with the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs, which suggested that the fluorescence of the CDs can be quenched by DAP through IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was measured to further explore the quenching mechanism. As presented in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was almost unchanged after adding DAP, indicating the IFE-based quenching mechanism. Further shown in Fig. S8, the absorption spectrum of CDs remained unchanged after the addition of DAP, which proved that no new substance was formed in the system. These results confirmed that DAP could quench the fluorescence of CDs through the IFE process.

On the basis of the IFE process between CDs and DAP, a ratiometric system was established for XOD analysis by catalytic oxidation of OPD to DAP with WO3 nanosheets. As displayed in Fig. 4D, OPD/XAN, OPD/XOD and OPD/XOD/XAN had a negligible influence on the fluorescence of CDs. In contrast, with the addition of OPD//XOD/XAN/WO3, the fluorescence of CDs at 430 nm displayed a remarkable decline and a new fluorescence emission peak of DAP emerged. The results suggested that the ratiometric fluorescent system was successfully constructed for the quantitation of XOD activity.

To achieve good performance in constructing the sensing system of XOD, several critical factors including reaction pH, concentration of the CDs, reaction temperature, and reaction time were optimized. The optimum parameters were estimated by calculating the value of fluorescence intensity ratio F570/F430. The influence of pH on the determination of XOD was studied in the range of 5.0–8.0 by using a 50 mM PBS buffer solution. As displayed in Fig. S9A, the fluorescence intensity ratio F570/F430 achieved the maximum value at pH 6.5, which was chosen as the optimum pH value. The influence of the concentration of CDs from 0.1 to 2.0 mg/mL was investigated. Fig. S9B manifested that the fluorescence intensity ratio F570/F430 achieved the maximum value at the concentration of 2.0 mg/mL. The influence of reaction temperature in the range of 15–40 °C was investigated. As displayed in Fig. S9C, the optimal reaction temperature was 35 °C. The influence of reaction time in the range of 10–50 min was studied. The results presented in Fig. S9D indicated that the reaction time of 30 min was sufficient to reach the maximum fluorescence intensity ratio.

Under the optimal conditions, the linear range and sensitivity of the established method were investigated by recording the fluorescence spectra of sensing system with different concentrations of XOD. The fluorescence intensity of CDs at 430 nm gradually increased with the increase of XOD concentration, while that of DAP at 570 nm gradually decreased. In addition, the fluorescence emission peaks of CDs exhibited a weak red-shift with the increasing of XOD concentration. We speculate that the red-shift is mainly owing to the enhancement of IFE between CDs and DAP, as well as differences in fluorescence quenching and energy conversion processes, which are related to the concentration of the sample (Fig. 5 A). The inset of Fig. 5B displayed the linear relationships between fluorescence intensity ratio F570/F430 and the activity of XOD in the range of 0.005–0.05 U/L (R2 = 0.9928) and 0.5–40 U/L (R2 = 0.9936), respectively, and the limit of detection (LOD) for XOD activity (defined as 3σ/K, σ represented the standard deviation of the blank signal for 11 replicate measurements, and K represented the slope of linear calibration curve) was 0.002 U/L. The obtained results were more sensitive than those of the early reported studies for XOD activity analysis (Table S2), indicating that the fluorometric method showed good sensitivity for XOD assay.

To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of this method, the interference effects of potential interfering substances including metal ions (Cd2+, Co2+, K+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cr2+、Mn2+, Mo6+, Ni2+), amino acids (Tyr, Gly, L-Cys, Lys), biomolecules (AA, Ade, Gua, UA, GSH, GLU, HSA, α-Glu, PPO), and H2O2 on the fluorescence intensity ratio F570/F430 were explored. As displayed in Fig. 5C, upon the addition of the above interfering substances, the fluorescence intensity ratio F570/F430 did not change significantly. The obtained results indicated the ratiometric system based on CDs and WO3 nanosheets had high selectivity and anti-interference ability for XOD analysis.

Allopurinol, a well-known XOD inhibitor and a drug for the treatment of hyperuricemia, was selected as a typical inhibitor to investigate the potential application of this assay in XOD inhibitor screening. The fluorescence response of this sensing assay towards allopurinol was exhibited in Fig. 6 A. It was clear that a gradual decrease of the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 430 nm and an associated enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 570 nm occurred as the increase of allopurinol in the range of 0.1–150 μM. The inhibition ratio (IR) was defined as IR (%) = [(Ii-I0)/(Ia-I0)] × 100, where Ia represents the initial fluorescence intensity ratio F570/F430 without XOD, and I0 and Ii represent the fluorescence intensity ratio F570/F430 without or with allopurinol, respectively. Fig. 6B showed that the IR constantly increased with the increase of allopurinol in amount. A good linear correlation (R2 = 0.9929) was shown under the logarithm of allopurinol concentration (from 0.1 to 150 μM). The LOD was estimated to be 1.8 nM for allopurinol (defined as 3σ/K). In addition, the IC50 value of allopurinol for XOD was determined to be approximately 10.47 μM from the standard curve. These results clearly indicated that the proposed approach was ultra-sensitive and could be applicable to XOD inhibitor screening in drug development.

Moreover, the XOD inhibitors extracted from medicinal plants were reported with low side effects and good therapeutic efficacy [48]. As displayed in Fig. S10, the IC50 values of the ethanol extracts from Tripterygium wilfordii Hook. f., Folium Nelumbinis, Terminalia chebula Retz., Dendranthema morifolium, Pueraria montana, Crataegus pinnatifida, and Curcuma phaeocaulis Valeton were 7.74, 9.36, 4.32, 6.89, 5.95, 5.12 and 6.25 mg/mL, respectively, which were in agreement with the previous research results [49]. Thus, the proposed approach could be used to screen trace XOD inhibitors from medicinal plants.

To further assess its practicability, the proposed method was employed to determine XOD in spiked normal human serum samples. As displayed in Table S3, the mean recoveries of XOD were 94.0–109.2% at a spiked level of 0.05–0.50 U/L, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the method were 1.6–3.8% (intra-day, n = 3) and 1.8–4.3% (inter-day, n = 9), respectively. Meanwhile, the XOD activity in serum samples of patients with hyperuricemia of different ages (15, 39, 48, 62, and 82 years old) was detected by comparing with standard colorimetric method by t-test. As listed in Table S4, the t-values fell below the critical value (2.776) at the 95% confidence level with 4 degrees of freedom, suggesting that the results determined by fluorescence method agreed well with those determined by standard colorimetric methods with respect to accuracy. These validation data manifested that the proposed method was appropriate for complex biological matrixes with satisfactory reliability.

In addition, using the established methods, we took the cohort of patients with hyperuricemia (8 men, 2 women, aged 15–82 years old) as an example and compared to the normal control group (6 men, 4 women, aged 22–76 years old) to measure the XOD value in a clinical range. As listed in Table 1 , XOD levels in the hyperuricemia group increased several times, which were clearly different from the normal control group. It is suggested that the level of XOD could be applied as the clinical indicator for diagnosis of hyperuricemia disease.

In summary, a novel ratiometric fluorescent system for XOD analysis and inhibitor screening was established based on CDs and WO3 nanosheets with peroxidase-like activity for the first time. The proposed method showed good linearities of XOD activity in the range of 0.005–0.05 U/L and 0.5–40 U/L and a low LOD of 0.002 U/L. Additionally, the practicability of the developed biosensing method was verified by the detection of XOD activity in human serum samples and screening of XOD inhibitors. This work not only provides a powerful tool for XOD-related assays based on new methods and new materials and could be expected to improve the performance of detection, but also exhibits great potential in disease diagnosis and drug development.

Xinxin Jiang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing-original draft. Weiping Liu: Project administration, Resources. Yue Li: Investigation, Methodology. Wanglisha Zhu: Investigation, Validation. Hongmei Liu: Investigation, Methodology. Yulu Wen: Investigation, Validation. Ruyu Bai: Investigation, Methodology. Xiaojun Luo: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Guoqi Zhang: Supervision, Project administration. Yan Zhao: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by west light foundation of the Chinese academy of sciences (to YZ), and the Science and Technology Programs of Sichuan Province of China (No. 2021YFS0060 and No. 2023NSFSC0637).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent and stable N-doped carbon dots as nanoprobes for Hg2+ detection
Nanomaterials
